correctAnswer: Fright from loud noises triggering cardiac arrest

statement: White-tailed eagles were extinct in Britain for nearly a century before being reintroduced from Norway in the 1970s — today they are thriving and have become the UK's largest bird of prey.
explanation: Persecution, egg collecting, and habitat loss drove white-tailed eagles to extinction in Britain by 1918. A painstaking reintroduction programme brought Norwegian birds to Scotland, and the population has since grown to over 150 breeding pairs.
wowFactor: Britain's biggest eagle came back from the dead after a century-long absence!
quizQuestion: When were white-tailed eagles reintroduced to Britain?
correctAnswer: 1970s, using birds from Norway

statement: Eurasian jackdaws are one of the few birds with pale, almost white irises that they use for social signalling — other jackdaws pay attention to where a pale-eyed individual is looking.
explanation: Research has shown that jackdaws follow gaze direction of other jackdaws by tracking their conspicuous pale eyes. This is similar to how humans use the whites of their eyes (sclera) for social communication, making jackdaws remarkably human-like in this regard.
wowFactor: These crows evolved human-like eye contact communication independently!
quizQuestion: What do Eurasian jackdaws use their pale irises for?
correctAnswer: Social signalling — others follow their gaze direction

distractors.text: Filtering light in bright snow conditions

statement: Little grebes can control their buoyancy by squeezing trapped air from their feathers, allowing them to slowly sink beneath the water's surface like a submarine rather than diving with a splash.
explanation: By compressing their plumage against their body, grebes expel air pockets and increase their density. This 'submarine sinking' makes them nearly invisible to predators and prey alike, as there is no splash or ripple to betray their position.
wowFactor: This tiny waterbird can turn itself into a submarine at will!

correctAnswer: By squeezing air from their feathers to reduce buoyancy

statement: American lobsters can live over 100 years and never stop growing — the largest ever caught weighed 20.1 kg (44.4 lbs) and was estimated to be over a century old.
explanation: Unlike most animals, lobsters exhibit 'negligible senescence' — they show no measurable decline in strength, appetite, or reproductive ability with age. They produce telomerase, an enzyme that repairs DNA, throughout their entire lives.
wowFactor: Lobsters basically refuse to age — they just keep growing bigger and stronger forever!

statement: Unlike the sociable white stork that nests on rooftops, the black stork is one of Europe's most secretive large birds — it nests deep in ancient forests and will abandon its nest if humans come within 300 metres.
explanation: Black storks are so sensitive to disturbance that forestry operations near their nesting sites are restricted by law in many European countries. A single pair requires several square kilometres of undisturbed old-growth forest to breed successfully.
wowFactor: Two stork species, opposite personalities — one loves your chimney, the other vanishes if you get within 3 football fields!

correctAnswer: It nests in remote forests and avoids humans

statement: The willow warbler weighs just 8-10 grams (lighter than two nickels) yet migrates up to 12,000 km from Scandinavia to sub-Saharan Africa — one of the longest migrations for any songbird its size.
explanation: These tiny birds nearly double their weight by gorging on insects before migration, storing fat as fuel. They fly mostly at night, navigating by stars and the Earth's magnetic field, and can cover 200+ km in a single night flight.
wowFactor: A bird that weighs less than a AAA battery commutes farther than most humans travel in a lifetime!

correctAnswer: Up to 12,000 km

statement: Sanderlings are the shorebirds you see frantically chasing waves on beaches — they run at the receding water to snatch tiny crustaceans, then sprint back up the sand to avoid the next incoming wave.
explanation: This distinctive 'wave-chasing' behaviour is so reliable that sanderlings are among the easiest shorebirds to identify by behaviour alone. They feed in the swash zone where waves deposit tiny sand crabs and marine worms, timing their runs with remarkable precision.

statement: The Siberian wood frog can survive being frozen solid at -35°C for months — its heart stops, blood freezes, and yet it thaws out alive in spring and hops away.
explanation: These frogs produce cryoprotectants (natural antifreeze) including glucose and urea that prevent ice crystals from destroying their cells. Up to 65% of their body water can freeze solid while the concentrated sugar solution inside their cells remains liquid.

statement: Glaucous gulls are apex predators of the Arctic that eat almost anything — including other seabirds, lemmings, fish, eggs, and even young Arctic foxes.
explanation: As one of the largest gulls in the world, glaucous gulls dominate bird colonies in the high Arctic. Their opportunistic diet and aggressive nature make them one of the most important predators in tundra ecosystems, capable of swallowing small birds whole.

statement: Silver-eared mesias live in flocks of up to 30 birds that forage in coordinated waves through bamboo forests, with members taking turns as sentinels watching for predators.

explanation: Pebbles are a scarce and valuable nesting resource in Antarctic penguin colonies. A well-chosen pebble demonstrates the male's ability to find resources. wowFactor: Penguin proposals involve the world's tiniest engagement ring — a carefully chosen pebble!
quizQuestion: How do male gentoo penguins court females?
correctAnswer: By finding and presenting the perfect pebble as a gift

distractors.text: By building the tallest snow fort
By fighting other males in front of her
By swimming the fastest in front of her

visualDescription: A male gentoo penguin with orange beak proudly presenting a smooth round pebble to a female penguin on a rocky Antarctic beach, both looking at the pebble, snowy mountains and colony in the background.

statement: American crows can recognize individual human faces and hold grudges for years — researchers who trapped and banded crows were mobbed by those same crows (and their offspring) more than 5 years later.
explanation: In a famous University of Washington experiment, researchers wore specific masks while trapping crows. Years later, crows who had never been trapped but had learned from their parents would scold and mob anyone wearing the 'dangerous' mask, demonstrating cultural transmission of enemy recognition.
wowFactor: Crows remember your face, teach their kids to hate you, and their grandkids will too!
quizQuestion: How long can crows remember and hold grudges against specific humans?
correctAnswer: More than 5 years, and they teach their offspring to recognise threats

distractors.text: About 6 months but they don't teach offspring
About 2 years but only the original crow
Indefinitely but they cannot distinguish faces

visualDescription: An American crow perched on a university campus lamppost, intensely staring down at a person below wearing a distinctive mask, several other crows gathering and cawing in alarm, autumn campus setting.

statement: Dragonflies catch prey mid-air with a 95% success rate by using predictive interception — they calculate where a target will be and fly to that point, rather than chasing. This is the same technique fighter pilots use.
explanation: Dragonflies are the most efficient aerial predators on Earth. Their brain processes visual information in just 30 milliseconds and adjusts wing angles independently (they have 4 independent wings) to intercept moving targets. They can hover, fly backwards, and reach speeds of 54 km/h.
wowFactor: Dragonflies use fighter pilot math to catch prey — and succeed 95% of the time, better than any other predator!
quizQuestion: What hunting technique makes dragonflies so successful?
correctAnswer: Predictive interception — flying to where prey will be, not where it is

distractors.text: Following prey from behind until it tires
Using echolocation to find prey in darkness
Herding insects into webs of other predators

visualDescription: A metallic blue dragonfly in sharp focus, wings frozen mid-beat showing all four independently angled, intercepting a mosquito with trajectory lines showing the calculated interception path, marsh habitat behind.

statement: Platypus fur glows blue-green under ultraviolet light — making the platypus one of very few mammals known to be biofluorescent, alongside some opossums and flying squirrels.
explanation: Discovered in 2020, this biofluorescence has no confirmed function yet. Since platypuses are nocturnal and crepuscular, some researchers speculate the glow may help them see or communicate in low UV light conditions at dawn and dusk, or it may simply be a byproduct of fur chemistry.
wowFactor: The duck-billed, venomous, egg-laying mammal ALSO glows in the dark under UV light — it breaks every rule!
quizQuestion: What colour does platypus fur glow under ultraviolet light?
correctAnswer: Blue-green

distractors.text: It does not glow at all

visualDescription: A platypus swimming in a dark pool, half the image showing normal brown fur under regular light, the other half bathed in UV light revealing bright blue-green fluorescent fur, Australian creek at night.
